reasoning: The finding is not valid for two independent reasons. First, the output mitigation: create_select_options builds $out and, when $output is true (the default), passes it through WPCaptcha_Utility::wp_kses_wf(), which calls wp_kses() with an explicit allowlist that includes <option> and its attributes. WordPress wp_kses() attribute-encodes values inside allowed attributes, defeating both attribute-breakout and script-injection attempts. Second, the reachability claim is false: all three actual call sites (tab_captcha.php line 53, tab_login_form.php line 232, tab_geoip.php line 31) pass only hardcoded developer-defined literal arrays — no data read from the database, GET/POST parameters, or any other attacker-controlled source ever reaches the $options parameter. The $tmp['val'] and $tmp['label'] values are constants like 'disabled'/'Disabled', 'builtin'/'Built-in Math Captcha', etc. There is no plausible attack path: neither the missing esc_attr()/esc_html() at the interpolation site nor a hypothetical injection-capable caller exists in the codebase.
